content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 19, 2019

Veronica Nebb
Sr. Assistant City Attorney
City of Novato
670 W. Napa Street, Suite F
Sonoma, CA 95476

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-002

Dear Ms. Nebb:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Novato Mayor Eric Lucan
regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note
that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under
other general conflict of interest prohibitions. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when
rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your
facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions
should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTIONS

Considering the additional facts presented, does the Act prohibit Mayor Lucan from taking
part in governmental decisions relating to:

(1) The City's potential provision of financial assistance to the Novato Theater Group for
the reconstruction of the Novato Theater, given that the Novato Theater is located approximately
300 feet from a residential rental property on Machin Avenue (the "Machin Property") owned by
the Mayor and his wife?

(2) A potential project (the "Depot Lot Project") to redevelop a 0.9-acre lot (the "Depot
Lot") located adjacent to the Sonoma-Marin Area Rail Transit ("SMART") station under
construction in downtown Novato (the "Downtown Station") given that the Depot Lot is located
837 feet from the Machin Property?

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-002
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

(1) Yes. The Act prohibits Mayor Lucan from taking part in decisions relating to the City's potential provision of financial assistance to the Novato Theater Group for the reconstruction of the Novato Theater because it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions will have a measurable impact on his real property interest the Machin Property.

(2) No. The Act does not prohibit the Mayor from taking part in decisions relating to the Depot Lot Project because it is not reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a material financial effect on his real property interest in the Machin Property.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You are a Senior Assistant City Attorney for the City of Novato and the authorized representative of Novato Mayor Eric Lucan. The Mayor owns the Machin Property, a residential duplex located on Machin Avenue within the City's downtown that he rents to tenants, and he has an interest in that property worth more than $2,000.

The Novato Theater and the Depot Lot are both within the City's "Downtown Core," an area in which the City permits a mix of retail, service, office, recreation, assembly, education, residential, and utility uses. The Machin Property is within an area of the City zoned "Commercial/Industrial," located immediately north of the eastern portion of the Downtown Core. The Machin Property is a permitted non-conforming residential use because it predates the commercial/industrial zoning of the surrounding area. The Mayor's residential rental property management business also owns other residential rental property located elsewhere in the City.

Potential Provision of Financial Assistance for the Reconstruction of the Novato Theater

The Novato Theater Group, a 501(c)(3) organization, owns the Novato Theater, a historic former movie house located in the City's downtown that has been shuttered since 1991. The Novato Theater Group has been reconstructing the Novato Theater for use as a 250-seat community theater, and recently had to halt the reconstruction due to insufficient funding. The Novato City Council will soon consider whether to provide financial assistance to the Novato Theater Group to enable the reconstruction to proceed.

On October 8, 2018, the Commission issued the Lucan Advice Letter, No. A-18-208, advising then-Councilmember Lucan that the Act prohibited him from taking part in decisions relating to the City's potential provision of financial assistance to the Novato Theater Group for the reconstruction of the Novato Theater because it was reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a measurable impact on his real property interest in the Machin Property under the Act's "500-foot rule," then set forth in Regulation 18702.2(a)(11). You have presented the following additional facts relating to those decisions, and have asked whether they would affect our determination that the Act prohibits the Mayor from taking part in those decisions.

In 2010, the City transferred the Novato Theater to the Novato Theater Group subject to the following conditions: (1) the property underlying the Novato Theater may only be used for events specified in the "Exclusive Event Listing for the Novato Theater," a document incorporated by

File No. A-19-002
Page No. 3

reference in the transfer; and (2) any work on any structure located on the property must be
consistent with the United States Secretary of the Interior's "Standards for the Treatment of Historic
Properties with Guidelines for Preserving, Rehabilitating, Restoring & Reconstructing Historic
Buildings," a federal government publication setting forth federal regulatory standards and industry
guidelines. Also, in making the transfer, the City withheld the right to expand structures on the
Novato Theater property higher than an adjacent parking lot.

Your analysis indicates that due to these transfer conditions and the City's withholding of
the right to airspace expansion: (1) uses of the property cannot be fundamentally changed from
those set forth in the "Exclusive Event Listing for the Novato Theater"; (2) the Novato Theater's
reconstruction is subject to historical limitations; and (3) the Novato Theater cannot be expanded
beyond the underlying property's boundaries.

The Machin Property is located approximately 300 feet northeast of the Novato Theater, and
includes a vacant area that can accommodate six to ten additional parked vehicles. This area is used
by tenants and their guests whenever additional parking is necessary.

The Depot Lot Project

SMART is a passenger rail service provided by a regional transportation district that was
established to oversee the development and implementation of passenger rail service in Sonoma and
Marin Counties. SMART provides rail service within the City, and there are currently two SMART
stations located within City limits. In May 2017, the City approved a "final funding plan" for a third
SMART station, the Downtown Station, which will be located at the "Historic Depot Site" in the
easternmost portion of the City's Downtown Core. The Depot Lot, the site of the Depot Lot Project,
is immediately adjacent to the Historic Depot Site.

On June 26, 2018, the Commission issued the Lucan Advice Letter, No. A-18-096. Because
the request for advice associated with that letter did not identify a specific governmental decision
relating to the then-inchoate Depot Lot Project, we were only able to provide the general advice that
the Act prohibited then-Councilmember Lucan from taking part in such a decision if it was
reasonably foreseeable that the decision would have a material financial effect on one or more of
his financial interests. You have presented the following additional facts relating to the Depot Lot
Project, and have asked whether those additional facts would affect our general advice provided in
that letter.

The City has recently requested proposals for Depot Lot Project, and has received two
proposals in response. The two proposals are substantially similar with respect to their proposed
uses of the Depot Lot. The first proposal would preserve the existing historic depot building and put
it to use as a small retail space or a local museum; create a public plaza with landscaping, paving,
and an area for food-truck parking with some permanent seating; establish an area for renovated
shipping containers or other modular buildings to be used by local small businesses; and set aside
an area nearby the Downtown Station for bike or scooter rentals.

File No. A-19-002
Page No. 4

The second proposal would preserve the existing historic depot building and put it to use as
a small, brewpub-type establishment; create an outdoor seating area near the historic depot building
with a covered roof, corrugated metal cladding, and open "family style" seating; establish an area
for renovated shipping containers to be used by another brewpub-type establishment and by other
eating establishments; and include a coffee shop, an area for bike rentals, and an area for commuter
or rideshare drop off or pick up.

The Depot Lot is located 837 feet southeast of the Machin Property, just east of the
easternmost portion of the City's Downtown Core. Those two properties are separated by several
blocks of the Downtown Core, and those blocks include multiple buildings of two or more stories.
The Depot Lot is not visible from the Machin Property.

U.S. Highway 101 is only approximately 160 feet east of the Depot Lot. You note that the
most direct route from U.S. Highway 101 to the Depot Lot would be to head west on DeLong
Avenue, then north on Reichart Avenue, and finally east on Grant Avenue. The entire route is east
of the Machin Property and does not pass nearby it.

Patrons of the redeveloped Depot Lot would have access to street parking and a parking
garage above a nearby Whole Foods Market which includes parking spaces available for the general
public. Both proposals for the Depot Lot Project envision SMART as the primary transportation for
getting to and from the redeveloped Depot Lot, and incorporate alternative transportation concepts,
such as on-site bike or scooter rentals, to reduce automobile-traffic impacts related to the Project.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on one or more of the official's financial interests distinguishable from the decision's effect
on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An official's interests that may give rise to a
disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are set forth in Section 87103. With respect to
decisions relating to the City's potential provision of financial assistance for the reconstruction of
the Novato Theater, or to decisions relating to the Depot Lot Project, Mayor Lucan has the
following interests based on the facts presented:

• A real property interest in the Machin Property because he has an interest in that property worth
more than $2,000. (See Section 87103(b).)

• A business interest in his residential rental property management business, if he either has an
investment in the business worth $2,000 or more (see Section 87103(a)), or is an employee of,
or holds any position of management with, the business (see Section 87103(d)).

• A source of income interest in his residential rental property management business, and in any
tenant of his residential rental properties, if he receives or is promised $500 or more from that
source in the 12 months prior to the decision at issue.2 (See Section 87103(c).)

_________________________
2 We do not analyze the effect of decisions relating to the City's potential provision of financial assistance for
the reconstruction of the Novato Theater, or decisions relating to the Depot Lot Project, on any source of income
interest that the Mayor may have in a tenant of the Mayor's residential rental property management business because the

File No. A-19-002
Page No. 5

• An interest in his personal finances or those of immediate family members. (See Section
87103.)

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's financial
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in
the decision at issue, and an interest is explicitly involved if the official's interest is a named party
in, or the subject of, the decision. Regulation 18701(b) sets forth the foreseeability standard
applicable to an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and provides that
the decision's effect on an official's inexplicitly involved interest is reasonably foreseeable if it
"can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical."

None of the Mayor's implicated interests are explicitly involved in any of the decisions at
issue. Therefore, with respect to those interests, the effect of each of those decisions is reasonably
foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or
theoretical.

Regulation 18702.2 provides the materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an official's real property interest. The Commission adopted
amendments to Regulation 18702.2 at its regular meeting on January 17, 2019, and the analyses
below apply that regulation as amended.

Potential Financial Assistance for the Reconstruction of the Novato Theater

Amended Regulation 18702.2(a)(7), the Act's "500-foot rule," provides that a decision's
reasonably foreseeable effect on an official's real property interest is material if the decision
"[i]nvolves property located 500 feet or less from the property line of the parcel unless there is clear
and convincing evidence that the decision will not have any measurable impact on the official's
property."

The Novato Theater is located approximately 300 feet southwest of the Machin Property,
and has been shuttered since 1991 when it ceased operating as a movie house. If the ongoing
reconstruction of the Novato Theater resumes due to the City's provision of financial assistance, it
will be substantially more likely that the reconstruction will be completed in a timely manner and
that the building will begin being used as a 250-seat community theater.

The reopening of the long-shuttered Novato Theater as a community theater, which will
regularly attract up to 250 attendees for events or performances, will increase the intensity of use of
the surrounding area. If events and performances resume at the Novato Theater, tenants of the
Machin Property are likely to experience increased automobile and foot traffic, additional noise,
and decreased privacy related to those events and performances due to being in the Novato
Theater's immediate vicinity. Furthermore, the Machin Property is unique within the Novato
Theater's surrounding area because it is a residential property that is a permitted non-conforming

you have not provided any facts regarding any particular tenant of that business. We note, however, that you should
seek further advice if there is any indication that any of those decisions may have a disqualifying effect on the Mayor's
source of income interest in a tenant of that business.

File No. A-19-002
Page No. 6

use within a commercial/industrial zone. An operating community theater that replaces a vacant
building within 300 feet of the Machin Property could add to the market value of that property
considering its unique zoning.

Your analysis indicates that due to restrictions on the property underlying the Novato
Theater resultant from the City's transfer of that property to the Novato Theater Group: (1) uses of
the property cannot be fundamentally changed from those set forth in the "Exclusive Event Listing
for the Novato Theater"; (2) the Novato Theater's reconstruction is subject to historical limitations;
and (3) the Novato Theater cannot be expanded beyond the underlying property's boundaries.
Although these restrictions may limit the Novato Theater's reconstruction, size and expansion, and
permitted performances and events, they do not support the conclusion that the Novato Theater's
timely reconstruction and reopening as a community theater would not have a measurable impact
on the Machin Property.

Therefore, based on all the facts presented, there is not clear and convincing evidence that
decisions relating to the potential provision of financial assistance for the reconstruction of the
Novato Theater will not have a measurable impact on the Machin Property. Thus, the Act prohibits
the Mayor from taking part in those decisions.³

Depot Lot Project

Business Interest in the Mayor's Residential Rental Property Management Business

Regulation 18702.3 provides the materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an official's interest in a source of income. Regulation 18702.3(a)(4),
however, provides that materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1, which sets forth
the materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on an official's business interest, if the
source of income is a business entity. Regulation 18702.5 sets forth the materiality standard
applicable to a decision's effect on an official's interest in his or her personal finances and those of
immediate family members. However, Regulation 18702.5(c) provides that materiality is
determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1 if the decision affects the official's business interest.

The Mayor has a source of income interest in his residential rental property management
business, as well as a business interest in that business, and the decisions relating to the Depot Lot
Project would affect that business. Therefore, we apply Regulation 18702.1 to determine whether
the decisions relating to the Depot Lot Project would have a disqualifying effect on the Mayor's
business interest in his residential rental property management business, source of income interest
in that business, and his interest in his personal finances.

Regulation 18702.1(b) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's privately-
held business interest is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find that it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision's effect will contribute to a change in the value of that

_____________
³ We do not analyze the effect of decisions relating to the City's potential provision of financial assistance for
the reconstruction of the Novato Theater on the Mayor's other financial interests because we have already determined
that those decisions would have a disqualifying effect on the Mayor's real property interest in the Machin Property.

⁴ See fn. 2.

File No. A-19-002
Page No. 7

business. The regulation enumerates examples of the types of decisions that may have a material
effect on a business under that standard, including those that:

(1) Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an
activity in which the business is engaged;
(2) Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which
the business entity is engaged;
(3) Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the
business entity supplies;
(4) Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as
redevelopment projects, traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that
may affect, either temporarily or permanently, the amount of business the
business entity receives;
(5) Decide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or
other project that would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity
draws from the location of the project; or
(6) Increase or decrease the tax burden, debt, or financial or legal liability
of the business entity.

There is no indication that the decisions relating to the Depot Lot Project would contribute
to a change in the value of the Mayor's residential rental property management business. With
respect to that business, those decisions are not described by any of the enumerated examples of the
types of decisions that would have a material effect under Regulation 18702.2(b). While either of
the proposals for the Project would significantly improve the Depot Lot and provide the
infrastructure for it to potentially become a thriving public plaza associated with the adjacent
Downtown Station, it does not appear that the Project would affect value of the Mayor's residential
rental property management business. The Downtown Core is already one of the City's primary
commercial areas, with an intensity of use commensurate with that status, and both Project
proposals have been designed to mitigate any increase in the area's intensity of use resultant from
the Project.

Because the decisions relating to the Depot Lot Project would not contribute to a change in
the value of the Mayor's residential rental property management business, it is not reasonably
foreseeable that those decisions would have a material financial effect on the Mayor's business
interest in that business.

Real Property Interest in the Machin Property

Under amended Regulation 18702.2(a)(8), a decision's effect on an official's real property
interest is material if the decision involves property located more than 500 but less than 1,000 feet
from the property line of the official's parcel, and the decision would change the parcel's market
value, development potential, income producing potential, highest and best use, or character by
substantially altering traffic levels, intensity of use, parking, view, privacy, noise levels, or air
quality.

The facts presented provide no indication that the decisions relating to the Depot Lot Project
would change the Machin Property's market value, development potential, income producing

File No. A-19-002
Page No. 8

potential, or highest and best use. Therefore, at issue is whether those decisions would change the
character of the Machin Property by substantially altering its intensity of use, traffic levels, parking,
view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality.

As noted above, the City's Downtown Core is already one of its primary commercial areas,
with an intensity of use commensurate with that status, and both Depot Lot Project proposals have
been designed to mitigate any increase in the area's intensity of use resultant from the Project. Both
proposals envision SMART as the primary transportation for getting to and from the redeveloped
Depot Lot, and incorporate alternate transportation concepts, such as on-site bike or scooter rentals,
to reduce automobile-traffic impacts related to the Project. Furthermore, because the entirety of the
most direct route from U.S. Highway 101 to the Depot Lot is east of the Machin Property and does
not pass nearby that property, the Project is unlikely to substantially alter automobile traffic in the
immediate vicinity of that property.

With respect to parking, patrons of the redeveloped Depot Lot would have access to street
parking and a parking garage above a nearby Whole Foods Market that includes parking spaces
available for the general public. Also, the Machin Property includes a vacant area used for tenant
and guest parking when additional parking is necessary that can accommodate six to ten additional
parked vehicles. Therefore, the Project is unlikely to substantially alter the parking available to
tenants of the Machin Property.

The Depot Lot is 837 feet southeast of the Machin Property, with several blocks of the
City's Downtown Core between the two properties, and those blocks include multiple buildings of
two or more stories. Because the Depot Lot is not visible from the Machin Property, the Project will
not affect that property's views. There is no apparent reason for automobile or foot traffic from the
redeveloped Depot Lot to venture into the immediate vicinity of the Machin Property, and the lack
of additional traffic in that vicinity indicates that the Project will not substantially alter the Machin
Property's privacy. Given that the Depot Lot is adjacent to the Downtown Station, and that U.S.
Highway 101 is only 160 feet east of the Depot Lot, any air or noise pollution from the redeveloped
Depot Lot is likely to be subsumed by the noise and air pollution from the Downtown Station and
U.S. Highway 101. Therefore, the Project is unlikely to substantially alter the Machin Property's
views, privacy, noise levels, or air quality.

Thus, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decisions relating to the Depot Lot Project
would have a material financial effect on the Mayor's real property interest in the Machin Property.
Accordingly, the Act does not prohibit him from taking part in those decisions.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,
Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Matthew N. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
Considering the additional facts presented, does the Act prohibit Mayor Lucan from taking 
part in governmental decisions relating to: 
(1) The City's potential provision of financial assistance to the Novato Theater Group for 
the reconstruction of the Novato Theater, given that the Novato Theater is located approximately 
300 feet from a residential rental property on Machin Avenue (the "Machin Property") owned by 
the Mayor and his wife? 
(2) A potential project (the "Depot Lot Project") to redevelop a 0.9-acre lot (the "Depot 
Lot") located adjacent to the Sonoma-Marin Area Rail Transit ("SMART") station under 
construction in downtown Novato (the "Downtown Station") given that the Depot Lot is located 
83 7 feet from the Machin Property?
sections.conclusion:
(1) Yes. The Act prohibits Mayor Lucan from taking part in decisions relating to the City's . 
potential provision of financial assistance to the Novato Theater Group for the reconstruction of the 
Novato Theater because it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions will have a measurable 
impact on his real property interest the Machin Property. 
(2) No. The Act does not prohibit the Mayor from taking part in decisions relating to the 
Depot Lot Project because it is not reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a 
material financial effect on his real property interest in the Machin Property.

management business also owns other residential rental property located elsewhere in the City.

would have a measurable impact on his real property interest in the Machin Property under the 
Act's "500-foot rule," then set forth in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l 1). You have presented the 
following additional facts relating to those decisions, and have asked whether they would affect our

The Depot Lot Project

the request for advice associated with that letter did not identify a specific governmental decision 
relating to the then-inchoate Depot Lot Project, we were only able to provide the general advice that 
the Act prohibited then-Councilmember Lucan from taking part in such a decision if it was

The City has recently requested proposals for Depot Lot Project, and has received two

sections.analysis:
The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a 
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on one or more of the official's financial interests distinguishable from the decision's effect 
on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An official's interests that may give rise to a 
disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are set forth in Section 87103. With respect to 
decisions relating to the City's potential provision of financial assistance for the reconstruction of 
the Novato Theater, or to decisions relating to the Depot Lot Project, Mayor Lucan has the 
following interests based on the facts presented: 
• 
A real property interest in the Machin Property because he has an interest in that property worth 
more than $2,000. (See Section 87103(b).) 
• 
A business interest in his residential rental property management business, if he either has an 
investment in the business worth $2,000 or more (see Section 87103(a)), or is an employee of, 
or holds any position of management with, the business (see Section 87103(d)). 
• 
A source of income interest in his residential rental property management business, and in any 
tenant of his residential rental properties, if he receives or is promised $500 or more from that 
source in the 12 months prior to the decision at issue.2 (See Section 87103(c).) 
2 We do not analyze the effect of decisions relating to the City's potential provision of financial assistance for 
the reconstruction of the Novato Theater, or decisions relating to the Depot Lot Project, on any source of income 
interest that the Mayor may have in a tenant of the Mayor's residential rental property management business because  
• 
An interest in his personal finances or those of immediate family members. (See Section 
87103.) 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's finan~ial effect on an official's financial 
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in 
the decision at issue, and an interest is explicitly involved if the official's interest is a named party 
in, or the subject of, the decision. Regulation 18701(b) sets forth the foreseeability standard 
applicable to an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and provides that 
the decision's effect on an official's inexplicitly involved interest is reasonably foreseeable if it 
"can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." 
None of the Mayor's implicated interests are explicitly involved in any of the decisions at 
issue. Therefore, with respect to those interests, the effect of each of those decisions is reasonably 
foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical. 
Regulation 18702.2 provides the materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect on an official's real property interest. The Commission adopted 
amendments to Regulation 18702.2 at its regular meeting on January 17, 2019, and the analyses 
below apply that regulation as amended. 
Potential Financial Assistance for the Reconstruction of the Novato Theater 
Amended Regulation 18702.2(a)(7), the Act's "500-foot rule," provides that a decision's 
reasonably foreseeable effect on an official's real property interest is material if the decision 
"[i]nvolves property located 500 feet or less from the property line of the parcel unless there is clear 
and convincing evidence that the decision will not have any measurable impact on the official's 
property." 
The Novato Theater is located approximately 300 feet southwest of the Machin Property, 
and has been shuttered since 1991 when it ceased operating as a movie house. If the ongoing 
reconstruction of the Novatp Theater resumes due to the City's provision of financial assistance, it 
will be substantially more likely that the reconstruction will be completed in a timely manner and 
that the building will begin being used as a 250-seat community theater. 
The reopening of the long-shuttered Novato Theater as a community theater, which will 
regularly attract up to 250 attendees for events or performances, will increase the intensity of use of 
the surrounding area. If events and performances resume at the Novato Theater, tenants of the 
Machin Property are likely to experience increased automobile and foot traffic, additional noise, 
and decreased privacy related to those events and performances due to being in the Novato 
Theater's immediate vicinity. Furthermore, the Machin Property is unique within the Novato 
Theater's surrounding area because it is a residential property that is a permitted non-conforming 
you have not provided any facts regarding any particular tenant of that business. We note, however, that you should 
seek further advice ifthere is any indication that any of those decisions may have a disqualifying effect on the Mayor's 
source of income interest in a tenant of that business.  
use within a commercial/industrial zone. An operating community theater that replaces a vacant 
building within 300 feet of the Machin Property could add to the market value of that property 
considering its unique zoning. 
Your analysis indicates that due to restrictions on the property underlying the Novato 
Theater resultant from the City's transfer of that property to the Novato Theater Group: (1) uses of 
the property cannot be fundamentally changed from those set forth in the "Exclusive Event Listing 
for the Novato Theater"; (2) the Novato Theater's reconstruction is subject to historical limitations; 
and (3) the Novato Theater cannot be expanded beyond the underlying property's boundaries. 
Although these restrictions may limit the Novato Theater's reconstruction, size and expansion, and 
permitted performances and events, they do not support the conclusion that the Novato Theater's 
timely reconstruction and reopening as a community theater would not have a measurable impact 
on the Machin Property. 
Therefore, based on all the facts presented, there is not clear and convincing evidence that 
decisions relating to the potential provision of financial assistance for the reconstruction of the 
Novato Theater will not have a measurable impact on the Machin Property. Thus, the Act prohibits 
the Mayor from taking part in those decisions. 3 
Depot Lot Project 
Business Interest in the Mayor's Residential Rental Property Management Business 
Regulation 18702.3 provides the materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect on an official's interest in a source of income. Regulation 18702.3(a)(4), 
however, provides that materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1, which sets forth 
the materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on an official's business interest, if the 
source of income is a business entity. Regulation 18702.5 sets forth the materiality standard 
applicable to a decision's effect on an official's interest in his or her personal finances and those of 
immediate family members. However, Regulation 18702.S(c) provides that materiality is 
determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1 if the decision affects the official's business interest. 
The Mayor has a source of income interest in his residential rental property management 
business, as well as a business interest in that business, and the decisions relating to the Depot Lot 
Project would affect that business. Therefore, we apply Regulation 18702.1 to determine whether 
the decisions relating to the Depot Lot Project would have a disqualifying effect on the Mayor's 
business interest in his residential rental property management business, source of income interest 
in that business, 4 and his interest in his personal finances. 
Regulation 18702.l(b) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's privately-
held business interest is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find that it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision's effect will contribute to a change in the value of that 
3 We do not analyze the effect of decisions relating to the City's potential provision of financial assistance for 
the reconstruction of the Novato Theater on the Mayor's other financial interests because we have already determined 
that those decisions would have a disqualifying effect on the Mayor's real property interest in the Machin Property. 
4 See fu. 2.  
business. The regulation enumerates examples of the types of decisions that may have a material 
effect on a business under that standard, including those that: 
(1) Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an 
activity in which the business is engaged; 
(2) Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which 
the business entity is engaged; 
(3) Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the 
business entity supplies; 
. 
( 4) Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as 
redevelopment projects, traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that 
may affect, either temporarily or permanently, the amount of business the 
business entity receives; 
(5) Decide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or 
other project that would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity 
draws from the location of the project; or 
(6) Increase or decrease the tax burden, debt, or financial or legal liability 
of the business entity. 
There is no indication that the decisions relating to the Depot Lot Project would contribute 
to a change in the value of the Mayor's residential rental property management business. With 
respect to that business, those decisions are not described by any of the enumerated examples of the 
types of decisions that would have a material effect under Regulation l 8702.2(b ). While either of 
the proposals for the Project would significantly improve the Depot Lot and provide the 
infrastructure for it to potentially become a thriving public plaza associated with the adjacent 
Downtown Station, it does not appear that the Project would affect value of the Mayor's residential 
rental property management business. The Downtown Core is already one of the City's primary 
commercial areas, with an intensity of use commensurate with that status, and both Project 
proposals have been designed to mitigate any increase in the area's intensity of use resultant from 
the Project. 
Because the decisions relating to the Depot Lot Project would not contribute to a change in 
the value of the Mayor's residential rental property management business, it is not reasonably 
foreseeable that those decisions would have a material financial effect on the Mayor's business 
interest in that business. 
Real Property Interest in the Machin Property 
Under amended Regulatioff 18702.2(a)(8), a decision's effect on an official's real property 
interest is material if the decision involves property located more than 500 but less than 1,000 feet 
from the property line of the official's parcel, and the decision would change the parcel's market 
value, development potential, income producing potential, highest and best use, or character by 
substantially altering traffic levels, intensity of use, parking, view, privacy, noise levels, or air 
quality. 
The facts presented provide no indication that the decisions relating to the Depot Lot Project 
would change the Machin Property's market value, development potential, income producing  
potential, or highest and best use. Therefore, at issue is whether those decisions would change the 
character of the Machin Property by substantially altering its intensity of use, traffic levels, parking, 
view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality. 
As noted above, the City's Downtown Core is already one of its primary commercial areas, 
with an intensity of use commensurate with that status, and both Depot Lot Project proposals have 
been designed to mitigate any increase in the area's intensity of use resultant from the Project. Both 
proposals envision SMART as the primary transportation for getting to and from the redeveloped 
Depot Lot, and incorporate alternate transportation concepts, such as on-site bike or scooter rentals, 
to reduce automobile-traffic impacts related to the Project. Furthermore, because the entirety of the 
most direct route from U.S. Highway 101 to the Depot Lot is east of the Machin Property and does 
not pass nearby that property, the Project is unlikely to substantially alter automobile traffic in the 
immediate vicinity of that property. 
With respect to parking, patrons of the redeveloped Depot Lot would have access to street 
parking and a parking garage above a nearby Whole Foods Market that includes parking spaces 
available for the general public. Also, the Machin Property includes a vacant area used for tenant 
and guest parking when additional parking is necessary that can accommodate six to ten additional 
parked vehicles. Therefore, the Project is unlikely to substantially alter the parking available to 
tenants of the Machin Property. 
The Depot Lot is 837 feet southeast of the Machin Property, with several blocks of the 
City's Downtown Core between the two properties, and those blocks include multiple buildings of 
two or more stories. Because the Depot Lot is not visible from the Machin Property, the Project will 
not affect that property's views. There is no apparent reason for automobile or foot traffic from the 
redeveloped Depot Lot to venture into the immediate vicinity of the Machin Property, and the lack 
of additional traffic in that vicinity indicates that the Project will not substantially alter the Machin 
Property's privacy. Given that the Depot Lot is adjacent to the Downtown Station, and that U.S. 
Highway 101 is only 160 feet east of the Depot Lot, any air or noise pollution from the redeveloped 
Depot Lot is likely to be subsumed by the noise and air pollution from the Downtown Station and 
U.S. Highway 101. Therefore, the Project is unlikely to substantially alter the Machin Property's 
views, privacy, noise levels, or air quality. 
Thus, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decisions relating to the Depot Lot Project 
would have a material financial effect on the Mayor's real property interest in the Machin Property. 
Accordingly, the Act does not prohibit him from taking part in those decisions.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Considering the additional facts presented, does the Act prohibit Mayor Lucan from taking 
part in governmental decisions relating to: 
(1) The City's potential provision of financial assistance to the Novato Theater Group for 
the reconstruction of the Novato Theater, given that the Novato Theater is located approximately 
300 feet from a residential rental property on Machin Avenue (the "Machin Property") owned by 
the Mayor and his wife? 
(2) A potential project (the "Depot Lot Project") to redevelop a 0.9-acre lot (the "Depot 
Lot") located adjacent to the Sonoma-Marin Area Rail Transit ("SMART") station under 
construction in downtown Novato (the "Downtown Station") given that the Depot Lot is located 
83 7 feet from the Machin Property?

CONCLUSION: (1) Yes. The Act prohibits Mayor Lucan from taking part in decisions relating to the City's . 
potential provision of financial assistance to the Novato Theater Group for the reconstruction of the 
Novato Theater because it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions will have a measurable 
impact on his real property interest the Machin Property. 
(2) No. The Act does not prohibit the Mayor from taking part in decisions relating to the 
Depot Lot Project because it is not reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a 
material financial effect on his real property interest in the Machin Property.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 February 19, 2019 Veronica Nebb Sr. Assistant City Attorney City of Novato 670 W. Napa Street, Suite F Sonoma, CA 95476 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-19-002 Dear Ms. Nebb: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Novato Mayor Eric Lucan regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. QUESTIONS Considering the additional facts presented, does the Act prohibit Mayor Lucan from taking part in governmental decisions relating to: (1) The City's potential provision of financial assistance to the Novato Theater Group for the reconstruction of the Novato Theater, given that the Novato Theater is located approximately 300 feet from a residential rental property on Machin Avenue (the "Machin Property") owned by the Mayor and his wife? (2) A potential project (the "Depot Lot Project") to redevelop a 0.9-acre lot (the "Depot Lot") located adjacent to the Sonoma-Marin Area Rail Transit ("SMART") station under construction in downtown Novato (the "Downtown Station") given that the Depot Lot is located 837 feet from the Machin Property? ___________________________ ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-19-002 Page No. 2 CONCLUSIONS (1) Yes. The Act prohibits Mayor Lucan from taking part in decisions relating to the City's potential provision of financial assistance to the Novato Theater Group for the reconstruction of the Novato Theater because it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions will have a measurable impact on his real property interest the Machin Property. (2) No. The Act does not prohibit the Mayor from taking part in decisions relating to the Depot Lot Project because it is not reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a material financial effect on his real property interest in the Machin Property. FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER You are a Senior Assistant City Attorney for the City of Novato and the authorized representative of Novato Mayor Eric Lucan. The Mayor owns the Machin Property, a residential duplex located on Machin Avenue within the City's downtown that he rents to
